ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — sweet biscuit, bear-shaped, commercially produced
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for bear-shaped sweet biscuit; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack subcategories is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Teddy Biscuit is approximately 25 g (about one small snack pack or roughly 10–12 individual bear-shaped pieces).

nutrient_source.title: Teddy Biscuit — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Teddy Biscuit (cr_id F11080071). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sweet Biscuits and Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for plain sweet biscuit (GI ~70)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Teddy Biscuit specifically. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for plain sweet biscuits / cookies (wheat flour, sugar, shortening base), which consistently return GI values in the 65–75 range. Glycemic load per 25 g serving = (70 × 16.72 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 11.7 g. Available carbohydrate per serving = (66.89 − 2.41) × 0.25 = 16.12 g; GL = (70 × 16.12) / 100 ≈ 11.3, rounded to 11.7 using full carbohydrate basis for conservatism.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (28.72 g/100 g) and moderate sodium (270 mg/100 g) with minimal fibre or potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Teddy Biscuits: the very high sugar load (28.72 g/100 g) independently elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention, and the 270 mg/100 g sodium provides no cardiovascular benefit; if consumed at all, restrict to no more than one small pack (25 g) on rare occasions and pair with a potassium-rich food.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (28.72 g/100 g) and high GI (70) with low dietary fibre (2.41 g/100 g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Teddy Biscuits are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: sugar at 28.72 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, the GI of 70 confirms rapid glucose absorption, and the low fibre content (2.41 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering — these biscuits should be avoided entirely or limited to 1–2 pieces on rare occasions, always paired with a protein source to partially blunt the glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~8 g/100 g from palm oil), estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g from shortening), and high refined sugar (28.72 g/100 g) contributing to atherogenic lipid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Teddy Biscuits: the estimated saturated fat of approximately 8 g/100 g from palm-oil shortening raises LDL cholesterol, the presence of trans fat (estimated ~0.1 g/100 g) is atherogenic at any dose, and the high refined sugar load promotes triglyceride elevation — consumption should be minimised or eliminated as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory combination of high refined sugar, saturated fat, and estimated trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Teddy Biscuits should be avoided by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis: the combination of very high refined sugar (28.72 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~8 g/100 g), and estimated trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) collectively promotes systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms — no component of this product provides anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teddy Biscuit is a commercially manufactured, bear-shaped sweet biscuit widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically sold in small snack packs of approximately 25 g. It is produced by baking a dough made from refined wheat flour, sugar, and vegetable shortening (commonly palm oil or a palm-oil blend), with salt and leavening agents. The result is a light, crisp, mildly sweet biscuit with a characteristic embossed bear shape that makes it popular as a children's snack and casual treat. Per 100 g, Teddy Biscuit provides 473 kcal, 6.22 g protein, 17.4 g fat (approximately 8 g saturated, estimated 0.1 g trans fat from shortening), 66.89 g carbohydrate, 28.72 g sugar, 2.41 g dietary fibre, and 270 mg sodium. Calcium and iron are reported as 0 mg/100 g in the HPB source. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high) based on analogous plain sweet biscuit data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 11–12 per 25 g serving. Clinically, Teddy Biscuit is a high-concern food for Type 2 Diabetes (very high sugar, high GI, low fibre), hypertension (high sugar with moderate sodium and no potassium offset), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat from palm-oil shortening), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory nutrient profile). It is classified as ultra-processed and is not suitable for individuals managing any of these conditions except in very small, infrequent portions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Teddy Biscuit?
Is Teddy Biscuit suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Teddy Biscuit?
Is Teddy Biscuit good for children?
How much fat is in Teddy Biscuit?
What is the GI of Teddy Biscuit?
What allergens are in Teddy Biscuit?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080071) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuits; this introduces minor uncertainty but the analogous match is strong. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from typical palm-oil shortening composition as the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions; these are clearly documented as estimates.
notes: 1. Calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per the HPB source (cr_id F11080071); these are confirmed zero values from the approved source and are not missing data. 2. Saturated_fat_g (~8.0 g/100 g) is estimated from the typical fatty acid profile of palm-oil-based vegetable shortening used in commercially produced sweet biscuits; the HPB record does not disaggregate fat fractions. Trans_fat_g (~0.1 g/100 g) is similarly estimated from typical shortening composition. These estimates are documented and flagged for verification against product packaging. 3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available in the HPB Foodo source for this record). 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain sweet biscuits; no direct GI entry exists for Teddy Biscuit. 5. Halal and kosher status are not confirmed from the HPB record; is_vegan is set to false as dairy-derived ingredients may be present in some formulations. 6. This food is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) due to its use of refined flour, added sugar, vegetable shortening, and industrial leavening agents.

improvement_actions_taken: Mapped input per_100g fields to RESULT template field names (e.g. energy → energy_kcal, carbohydrate → carbohydrate_g).
Added saturated_fat_g (estimated ~8.0 g from palm-oil shortening) and trans_fat_g (estimated ~0.1 g) with documentation in notes.
Set monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, and all unreported micronutrients to -1 with documentation.
Confirmed calcium_mg and iron_mg as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source) and documented in notes.
Replaced input GI source 'Embedded GI Database' with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate, fully documented.
Restructured health_suitability into health_context with required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) plus Rheumatoid Arthritis as additional.
Renamed 'diabetes' condition key to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.
Added ontology section with defensible FoodOn and FoodEx2 entries.
Built full aliases and aliases_by_language with consistency check passed.
Applied G6 multilingual compliance check: Teddy Biscuit is a generic international commercial snack, not a cuisine-specific dish requiring mandatory non-English names.
